title: Display deployed build identity in the application footer
objective: Make the currently deployed application version immediately identifiable by displaying the deployment timestamp, exact source commit and, when available, the associated pull request at the bottom of the application.
userContext: The application is deployed frequently while its features and delivery pipeline are evolving. The user needs to confirm at a glance whether the website is running the expected deployment without opening GitHub, Cloudflare or GitHub Actions.
currentBehaviour: The deployed application does not expose any build or deployment identity. It is not possible to determine from the website when it was deployed, which commit is running or which pull request introduced the deployed version.
requiredBehaviour: Every deployed application build must expose immutable deployment metadata and render a visually unobtrusive footer showing the deployment timestamp, abbreviated commit SHA and optional associated pull request number. The metadata must describe the exact source commit used by the production build. Local development must use a clear development-build fallback.
userExperience: A small, low-contrast deployment identity line is shown at the bottom of every application page. A typical production display is: `Deployed 12 Jul 2026, 11:24 BST · PR #54 · 3b30700`. The timestamp is displayed in the browser's local timezone while retaining its canonical UTC value. The pull request and commit identifiers link to their corresponding GitHub pages. When no pull request can be resolved, the footer omits that element without presenting an error. In local development, it displays `Development build`.
functionalRequirements: Generate deployment metadata during the production deployment workflow before the application worker build.
Include a canonical UTC deployment timestamp generated immediately before the production build.
Include the complete commit SHA of the exact repository commit checked out, verified and deployed.
Resolve the pull request associated with the deployed commit when one is available.
Treat the pull request number as optional because manual or recovery deployments may not correspond to a pull request.
Pass the exact commit SHA explicitly through the main orchestration and reusable deployment workflow rather than deriving it implicitly from an arbitrary checkout state.
Make deployment metadata available to the application at build time without committing generated deployment-specific data to the repository.
Expose deployment metadata through an explicit typed application contract.
Render a deployment identity footer on every application page.
Display the deployment timestamp in the user's browser-local timezone.
Retain the canonical UTC timestamp in the rendered markup or accessible metadata.
Display an abbreviated commit SHA while retaining access to the complete SHA.
Link the pull request number to the repository pull request when it is available.
Link the commit SHA to the corresponding repository commit.
Display `Development build` when deployment metadata is unavailable in local development.
Ensure the footer remains readable in both light and dark themes.
Keep the deployment footer visually secondary and avoid adding significant vertical space or visual noise.
technicalConsiderations: Prefer generating a typed module or JSON metadata file in the CI workspace before `npm run build:worker`; do not commit the generated deployment metadata.

Build-time environment variables may be used as workflow inputs, but application access should be wrapped in an explicit validated metadata contract rather than scattered direct environment-variable reads.
The deployment timestamp represents the production build/deployment start time and may differ slightly from Cloudflare publication completion time.
Do not introduce a second deployment solely to record the exact Cloudflare completion timestamp.
The main push event does not directly contain a pull request number, so resolve the associated PR through the GitHub API using explicit repository and commit context.
PR resolution must be resilient when no PR is associated with the commit.
The workflow must continue using the ephemeral GITHUB_TOKEN and must not introduce a personal access token.
Production secrets must remain restricted to the deployment workflow and must not be exposed to application code or PR validation workflows.
The deployed commit metadata must correspond to the exact commit verified by the main lifecycle workflow.
Generated metadata must not make local builds or tests dependent on GitHub availability.
Add deterministic test fixtures or dependency injection for metadata rendering.
Use semantic HTML suitable for a site-wide footer and maintain accessibility for links and timestamps.
outOfScope: Displaying Cloudflare credentials or sensitive deployment configuration.
Persisting deployment history in a database.
Showing detailed GitHub Actions or Cloudflare diagnostics in the normal footer.
Triggering a second application deployment to record the exact publication-completion time.
Changing the general visual design of the application footer beyond what is needed for deployment identity.
acceptanceCriteria: A production deployment displays a footer containing a deployment timestamp and abbreviated commit SHA.
The displayed commit SHA corresponds to the exact commit checked out and deployed by the production workflow.
The full commit SHA is available through the commit link, accessible text or tooltip.
When the deployed commit is associated with a pull request, the footer displays its PR number.
The PR number links to the correct pull request.
The commit identifier links to the correct GitHub commit.
When no associated pull request is found, deployment succeeds and the footer renders without a PR element.
The timestamp is stored canonically as UTC and displayed using the browser's local timezone.
The footer renders correctly in both light and dark themes.
The footer remains visually unobtrusive and does not materially disrupt existing page layouts.
Local development without generated deployment metadata displays `Development build`.
Tests cover production metadata rendering, missing optional PR metadata and the development fallback.
PR verification continues to run without production secrets.
The deployment workflow generates metadata before the worker build and deploys the same verified commit.
Generated deployment metadata is not committed to the repository.
The Next.js build and OpenNext Cloudflare worker build both succeed.
Relevant application specifications and deployment documentation are updated.
codexGuidance: Implement this later as one small vertical feature spanning deployment metadata generation, a typed application metadata contract and a shared footer component. Reuse the main-orchestrator and reusable-deployment architecture. Resolve the optional PR number before invoking the production build, pass the exact verified SHA explicitly and ensure feature rendering remains deterministic in tests and local development.
